title: practical english usage - Part 361
content:
What time does the match start? (More natural than At what time.. .?) . .. at another time.) He's busy. Why don't you come another time? (More natural than Prepositions are often dropped before some common expressions with time.

2 without      preposition not It's time to stop, (     It^s-the-time-tostopr) The is dropped in the expression it's time.

She phoned me at various times yesterday.

Six o'clock would be a good time to meet.

When  we  talk about clock times, time is countable.

For the use of take with expressions of time, ►     (>02.

Just come with me - I haven't got (the) time to explain.

The time can be used to mean 'enough time’ ; the is often dropped.

I took a long time to get to sleep. She was away for quite a time.

quite a time (BrE).

However, time is countable in certain expressions like a long/short time and Don't worry - there's plenty of time. This is a complete waste of time.

It took quite some time to persuade her to talk to us.

How much  time do we need to load the van?

something, time is generally uncountable (and therefore used without a).

When  we  talk about the number of hours, days, etc that are needed to complete a  measure of duration: how  long in two areas: see a good dictionary). Most of these are straightforward, but there are problems Time has various uses, some countable and some uncountable (for full details 1  countability         and   article     use 608 time and           ►   357 through,        across          over end of September.) or The park is open from May to September inclusive, (   . .. from May until the people say, for example, to ... inclusive, or until the end of...

In British English, through is not normally used in this way. Instead, British Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 609 vocabulary •         tonight far too young           rather too often a lot too big           a bit too soon not much  too old (     very too old) a little too confident Expressions which  modify comparatives (►  207) also modify too.

much       too,    far    too, 3 modification: etc I've got too much  work.

Too much  is used, for example, before nouns. For details, ►    611.

I arrived too early, (     I arrived too much  early.) not You're too kind to me. (     You're too much  kind to me.) Before adjectives without nouns and before adverbs we  use too, not too much.

2  too    and   too    much It was too cold to go out, so we stayed at home.

- It was very cold, but we went out.

He's too intelligent for his class - he's not learning anything.

- He's a very intelligent child.

necessary' or ‘ more than is wanted'. Compare: Too is different from very - too means 'more than enough', 'more than 1  too    and   very 610 too I hope / sleep better tonight.

I had a terrible dream last night. (     I had a terrible dream tonight.) Compare: Tonight refers to the present or coming night, not to the past night [last night).

and           ►   ɜ 85 to      at/in For tenses with this is the first time..., this is the last time... and similar structures, ►    56.

For structures after It's time, ►     3()2.   For ways of telling the time, ►    325.   For by the time,  ►     414.

I nearly drove into the car in front, but I stopped just in time.

(N     . . . got him  to the hospital on time.) He would have died if they hadn't got him  to the hospital in time.

We arrived in time to get good seats, (     . .. on-time to get good seats.) The  opposite is too late.

In time means 'with enough time to spare', 'before the last moment'.

Daniel wants the discussion to start exactly on time. (     . . .in time.) not Only one of the last six trains has been on time. (     .. .in time.) 'early' or 'late'. It is often used to refer to timetabled events.

On  time means 'at the planned time', 'neither late nor early'. The  opposite is 3 on   time     and   in   time 610 vocabulary  •         too and               ►   ɜe,g too,    also        as   well For too meaning ‘ also', ►    369.

We will be only too pleased if you can spend a few days with us.

formal offers and invitations.
